title: Webinar: The Ceasefire And Mad Markets
text:
Webinar: The Ceasefire And Mad Markets

Louis-Vincent Gave, Anatole Kaletsky

Gavekal Research

| 19 Jun 2026

The arrival of a US-Iran deal that would reopen the Strait of Hormuz has the potential to materially reshape the investment environment that has prevailed since February 28. Against a backdrop of artificial intelligence companies seeking to raise massive amounts of capital from investors, Louis and Anatole convene a conversation to explore what comes next.

This transcript is auto generated.
Louis-Vincent Gave
Good evening. Good morning. Good afternoon. Good morning, wherever you may be. Welcome to this latest Gavekal Zoominar. My name is Louis-Vincent Gave. I'm one of the founding partners of Gavekal. With me today, I'm delighted to welcome my co-founding partner, Anatole Kolecki. And to be honest, when we planned to do this call about 10 days ago, we thought we'd do it around the very large amounts being raised by SpaceX, by Google, the upcoming Anthropic and OpenAI IPOs, and how the markets seem to be getting overly excited around a fairly narrow theme. But, events being what they are, our plan was, of course, overtaken by all the developments in the Middle East. And for those of you who haven't read it, I highly, highly recommend the piece we published on Tuesday, the Tuesday's Daily, Anatole's Take on the Crisis. Since then, of course, we've had the signature of a peace deal in Versailles, of all places. It's a peace deal that includes massive reparations in Versailles. It's almost as if no one in the White House has either superstitious or read a history book before. And so, we'll obviously kick off with that.
But before we get started, I want to remind everyone that this call is being taped and will be released on our website, typically in about 24 hours. I also want to remind everyone to please ask us questions, and that these questions should be submitted through the Q&A box, not the chat box. So, if you look at the bottom of your screen, there's a Q&A box and a chat box. The chat box is really there for you if you have technical issues. Our producer, Tom, is monitoring those. And if you have issues with sound or if you need anything in particular, Tom will help you with those. And by the way, thanks, Tom. And otherwise, yes, the Q&A button, I will be monitoring those and either answering them directly by text or asking them to Anatole. So, and then finally, I want to remind everyone that the Zoominar is part of our provision of independent research on macroeconomics and markets, that we make no security-specific recommendations. We may talk about some stocks, but more as an example than any stock recommendation. There will also be no discussion of any confidential, restricted, or material non-public information.
So, these disclosures aside, Anatole, let me jump right into it. And here, I have to put my hand up because literally about last weekend, I was having a chat with my dad, Charles. And Charles was telling me, you know, he was saying, I think this Iran thing is coming to an end. And I said, there's no way it's coming to an end because Iran is insisting on massive reparations. And there's no way that the U.S. will pay reparations because if you pay reparations, that mean you lost the war. So, there's absolutely no way that this is going to happen. And, yeah, and then 48 hours later, here I am with egg all over my face. So, let's kick off with that because I think this is obviously a super, super important development. It's a very important development for the Middle East. It's a very important development for global markets. Do you want to briefly rehash what you said in your piece for perhaps the people who didn't have a chance to read it?
Anatole Kaletsky
Okay. Well, thank you, Louis. And you're a little too flattering, you know, that saying that just two pages or three pages on Tuesday, you know, was so revelatory. I mean, actually, because all I'm saying is that this is a case where I think you just have to believe what the official story is that's being presented by the two governments and actually not try to be too clever about, you know, anticipating the story behind the story and why the headlines are going to be wrong. The headlines this time are, the war is over. Both the principal antagonists, the Iranians and the U.S., clearly have decided they really want to end the war. There is a third one, Israel, which would clearly love to continue with the war. But it's very much not one of the stars in the show. It's part of the supporting cast. And as Trump said, you know, perhaps over explicitly a couple of days ago, you know, the U.S. is the senior partner and Israel is very much a junior partner. They would like to sabotage this deal if they possibly can. But I can't see any way that they'll be able to if you assume that America really wants to stop this war.
And this is where actually I would slightly differ with what you said, you know, about how difficult it was for the U.S. to agree to this with the reparations and so on. It wasn't difficult. There are no reparations going to Iran from the U.S. government. I think Iran is going to get a lot of investment from some of it from the U.S. private sector, but most of it from neighboring Gulf states. And if they want to treat that as reparations, that's fine. But basically what the U.S. government has done is return to the status quo ante not just before the war, but essentially back to the status quo as it was in up to 2016-17, whenever it was that the first Trump administration decided to back out of the so-called GCPOA agreement that Obama has negotiated. And they've negotiated a different version of it. And, you know, we shouldn't be too astonished by this. This is exactly what happened, after all, in our field and much less publicized, localized economic field of NAFTA.
You know, Trump swept NAFTA away and came up, in that case, only a few months later, in this case, five or six years later, he came up with the USMCA, which is essentially very, very similar to NAFTA. I think that is what the U.S. has now done. And the reason I think this is so important, you know, we can discuss it in more detail in a minute, you know, the geopolitics and why I might be wrong and so on. But if I'm right, that this war is now over, the reason this is so important to investors is that in the 15 months before the war, essentially between the Trump election in November 24 and the end of February 26, some very powerful trends got established in financial markets, two of which, at least, were extremely contrarian trends relative to what the world expected in November 24. And they were the rotation of capital out of the U.S. into Europe and Asia, despite Trump's promise to make America great again. As I said repeatedly last year, and it was a theme that many of us took up, the surprise was actually the dollar wasn't the strongest currency in the world, as nearly everybody expected. It was the weakest currency.
The U.S. stock market went up, but it wasn't the strongest market in the world. It was actually the weakest market in the world. It underperformed both Europe and Japan by about 20 percentage points. You're a massive, you're the biggest relative underperformance that we've seen in the U.S. since the beginning of this century. And so you had this rotation from the U.S. People called it the end of U.S. exceptionalism, but it wasn't a capital flight out of the U.S. It was just a reassessment by the global investment community about just how overweight they wanted to be, the U.S. Everybody wanted to be overweight, but even at 50 percent, you're overweight, the U.S., and 70 percent turned out to be too high. So that was perhaps the most surprising trend of that pre-war period. The other big trend with the pre-war period, which, again, people didn't really recognize till just the last few months, was a very, very big rotation out of growth into value, out of assets, especially in the U.S., but also in Asia, which had reached very, very high valuations on the basis of the tech AI theme. And they didn't do badly, but they went sideways.
And the things that really performed in the six, nine months before the war broke out were the deep value stocks, the things, the sectors and the assets that had been really underpriced for the previous five years because of the fears of a global recession and so on. And they were repricing upwards very, very rapidly because it was clear that the risk of a global recession was over. So that was the story up to February 27th. The other big trend, which people did understand and wasn't widely expected, was higher interest rates and higher inflationary expectations. Now, my view is that the world is now basically going back pretty much from a financial standpoint, pretty much to where it was on February 27th before the war broke out. And therefore, the trends that really got going in the previous 15 months are likely to resume.
Now, they're likely to resume from an investment standpoint from a much more attractive level, because what's happened in the last three months since the war broke out is there's been a massive countertrend correction, both in U.S. relative to the rest of the world and in technology relative to every other sector. So I think if the previous trends get re-established, this is a really big investment opportunity. And it's now, again, a contrarian one, as it was, you know, in November 24 when Trump was elected, rather than just being a consensus view, which it had become by February. So that's why I think whether this war really is over is very important. And the reason I think it really is over is that Trump clearly wants it over. He's the one who's backed down. And that actually is pretty good news for the world economy, because if it had been Iran that had kind of backed out, then you could imagine, well, they might start the war again. You know, maybe they're unsatisfied or maybe they'll start. But if it's the U.S. that has shown very, very clearly that they don't want to get involved, the chances of I know around.
I know Trump said, oh, we'll start bombing if we don't like it, et cetera. That's not going to happen, because he is thanking his lucky stars that he never has to think about Iran or the Middle East again. And that's the situation that he finds perfectly satisfactory, I think. The MAGA people love it. J.D. Vance loves it. Of course, all the neocons hate it. The Israelis hate it. But at the moment, and for the next few months, I don't think they have any agency in this.
Louis-Vincent Gave
I fully agree with this. In any event, I think electorally, it's not obvious to me that the neocons represent that big a segment of the U.S. voters. So, look, I agree with you that the big question for the coming quarters and years is whether we're restarting a period of rotation of capital out of the U.S., whether this is the starting gun for such a period, I think odds are it may very well be. And so we have a number of questions basically along those lines, essentially questions on the outlook for metals, on the outlook for gold, on the outlook for commodities in general, on the outlook for energy. I thought perhaps we would start off with energy because at the end of the day, we are talking about the Middle East. And there's a few questions on this outlook for energy. And, you know, I think when I – I'll just make a quick point, but you and I may have somewhat of a different view here. But for the way I look at things, it's – if we look at the lessons learned in this crisis, you know my pet theory that essentially China increasingly controls the oil price.
And it controls the oil price because it has this huge, huge inventory of oil, which allows China essentially – what you've seen really in the past decade is each time oil gets close to 60 bucks, 65, 60, China ships in as much as it can.
You see this in the import data. You see this in the cost of the freighters. You know, there's not enough ships. When oil is below 60, 65, there aren't enough ships to bring it into China, essentially. And then when oil gets close to 100 bucks, China just stops buying. And you saw this very clearly in 22 with the Ukraine thing, and you saw it again here, which leaves you with an oil price. This is right. This theory is right. It leaves you with an oil price that is essentially in this band between 60 bucks and 100 bucks because the biggest buyer in the market buys and sells at those levels, and in between sort of bumps around. And I actually think that this band is tremendously bullish energy stocks. And this is where I think I get a lot of pushback and discussions with clients because a lot of people buy energy stocks on the hope that oil goes to 150 bucks. The reality is between 60 and 100 bucks, you talk to any oil management, any oil company management, and they tell you, yeah, 60 to 100, we're very happy. Like, we make money like bandits.
And even better is between 60 and 100, they're actually decently disciplined with capital, which is always the curse of the oil companies, right? It's like when oil goes very high, they all get FOMO, and they all start drilling holes everywhere. Between 60 and 100 bucks, they actually buy back shares, and they give out big dividends. And which brings me to something that few people realize, but over the past five years, energy stocks have done almost as well as tech stocks. Their second best performing sector on the S&P 500. And better yet, they typically delivered performance when everybody else wasn't. So most of the outperformance came in 22 and again this year. And so I think we live in this world on energy where we're essentially stuck between 60 to 100, which, by the way, is a level pretty much everybody can live with. It's the level that the companies definitely can live with. But most countries, most consumers can live with that level. When you get to close to 100, it might get a little uncomfortable for the poor countries, the Sri Lankas, the Pakistan of this world, etc.
Which, incidentally, is another reason to think maybe we don't go much beyond that, because a lot of the marginal demand for oil does come from emerging markets. That's where the growth for oil is. But so if we're in that 60 to 100 range, and that becomes predictable, and the market realizes, actually, we're going to be 60 to 100 for a long time, I think this is actually pretty bullish growth globally, because it allows you to project yourself further. It allows you to not look over your shoulder and be too worried. Having said that, I do tend to believe that, obviously, during this crisis, everybody drew down their inventories to zero. So we're now going to be in a phase where everybody's going to have to rebuild inventories for a while. So all the hopes that oil prices are going to collapse from here, for me, are a little bit diluted. And maybe that's where you and I are a bit different, and where you might have a different outlook, or perhaps not.
Anatole Kaletsky
Well, no, I think that I would add two elements of agreement, and then I'll say where I disagree. So first of all, I think your first point about China really being the pricemaker in the oil market now is a really important one. And I think you're one of the first to talk about it, but that idea is catching on. In fact, just yesterday, there was a very good piece on Bloomberg making exactly that point, that it used to be that Saudi Arabia, being the biggest oil producer, Europe was the ultimate price setter and the regulator of the market. But now China, being by far the biggest oil consumer and importer, is in a position to regulate the market, and perhaps is beginning to conclude that it's in its interest to behave in such a way as to stabilize the market, as you said, now, whether it's between 60 and 100, or 70 and 90. But stability and a reasonable price for oil is actually very much in the Chinese interest as well. And they now do have the power, and I think will even more in the next year or two, because there'll be an excess of supply, have the power to regulate the market.
I also agree with you that they will not think it's in their interest just to allow the price to crash completely to $50, $40, $30. Apart from anything else, they are in a very important geopolitical alliance with Russia. They do still see themselves in a geopolitical conflict with the US and Europe, or at least a very intense geopolitical competition. And Russia is an important ally, so they don't want to bankrupt Putin either. So I agree with all that. And I think you're right that this kind of range trading is pretty comfortable and pretty healthy for most energy producers. The only other elements that I would add is, number one, where I think you possibly disagree, and Charles certainly disagrees, this is also a really good world for alternative energy, ranging from nuclear all the way down to, you know, which everybody loves these days, everybody in market loves and Trump loves and so on, and coal maybe even to some extent to the things that Trump hates, wind and solar. This is a very, very good environment for them because, again, it's stability.
The price is high enough that marginal cost of producing electricity from alternatives is clearly going to be cheaper than it is from any fossil fuel as long as prices stay in this range. Of course, the capital costs, you know, are substantial, so it's sensitive to interest rates, but also because the risk, you know, when energy prices go too high, and this applies, I think, to fossil fuel producers as well, as you said, if they go to 120, 130, 150, first, they suppress, you know, demand through substitution, but they also crush the world economy, as you said, you know, so there's a recession risk, and the third thing is that you start getting windfall taxes because as soon as the price gets beyond a certain level, you know, the governments all over the world say, well, you know, where can we raise money? So, I think this is very good news for alternative energy suppliers as well, and there, you know, while the asset prices of the fossil fuel companies, as you say, since 22, have done, you know, extremely well, the asset prices of all the alternative energy providers had collapsed until about a year ago. They've recovered a lot.
They've done very well in the last 12 months, but I think they actually have more upside than the fossil fuel companies themselves. The other area which I think is underestimated is the oil service and energy service businesses because there, you are going to have a massive reallocation of infrastructure that occurs. You know, we know that they're going to be building pipelines that eventually will make the straight and four moves pretty much irrelevant, but that will be five years. Iran, if I'm right, and this piece really does hold, Iran's going to be rebuilt. That's where the euro 300 billion is really going to be spent on and coming from. So I think there's a lot of interesting opportunities there, which in some ways I think may be safer and more reliable than the straight fossil fuel producers. And the last point I'd make is, well, why isn't this being priced in? And actually, you know, why is so much of what we're talking about like a return to the pre-war status quo? Why isn't it being priced in?
And I think the reason it certainly hasn't been in the last few days and probably won't be for the next few weeks is that most of the investment community doesn't believe that this war is over. and there is still a very, very, you know, a strong bias to say, oh, well, it can't be, you know, simple as this. There's going to be, they're going to start fighting all over again in two months' time and three months' time. And in my experience of watching the markets, you know, for the last 40 years or longer, I'm sorry to say, I think this kind of skepticism, when you have a really big game-changing transformation in the world, the skepticism in markets and in the media is invariably an aspect of that. So, you know, if you think back to 2008, when Bear Stearns went past, everybody's out, it doesn't matter, it's just one broker. It took two or three events of that kind, Fannie Mae, you know, going into receivership, and then Lehman for the market really to start believing.
And then on the upside, when it was all fixed by the combination of TARP and QE, nobody believed, literally almost nobody except for, I think, you, me, and Charles, and a few others, believed that this might actually be the end of the bear market. Because once you throw that much money into the economy, things turn around. So I think this is a slightly similar situation. People can't believe that it's as simple as signing an MOU, but I think in this case, it may be.
Louis-Vincent Gave
Well, on this, another example would be 2022 and the Russian invasion of Ukraine, where, you know, we all immediately saw, and we did this, we wrote about it, and we did calls on how, okay, the freezing of treasuries means that central banks are going to be buying gold hand over fist, load up on gold. And gold didn't really start going up until two or three months after the start of the Ukraine, right, and the freezing of the reserves. For a while, gold was still going down. And I remember looking at it, I'm like, what am I missing here? Like, you know what? Like, I'm an idiot. It's, well, you know, do I read this all wrong? One quick point. By the way, I, sorry.
Anatole Kaletsky
Well, another, while you're talking of summer of 22, another example of this is that actually bond yields did go up. They did shoot out of the previous trading range, as we, you know, I've been saying to clients for the last four years, but actually nobody believed, almost nobody believed that that was more than a blip. Everybody continued to believe that the new normal would be a return to the pre-COVID, pre-Ukraine situation of bond yields below 3%. And frankly, you know, even at the Fed, they continued to believe that until just a few months ago. So I think that's another very good example of just how long it takes, even when the data moves and some of the markets move in a certain direction, how long it takes for the consensus, opinion in politics and policymaking and the markets to recognize that the world has changed.
Louis-Vincent Gave
Sorry. No, no, no, not at all. Look, I, by the way, I don't disagree with you on the alternatives. I think a lot of people are looking at this crisis and sort of looking with envy at China's energy policy of the past decade. And China's energy policy of the past decade was very simple. It was simply more of everything. It was like, we want more coal and we want more solar and we want more nuclear and we want more oil and we want more nat gas. We just want more of everything. And we'll throw money at this problem and we'll do a lot of capital spending in consequence. And I do think during this crisis, you know, typical example here is India. India came into this crisis with 700 billion of U.S. treasuries three years ago, having essentially prevented Chinese solar panels and Chinese cheap EVs from coming into India with massive, massive tariffs. And I think if you're a Modi on day two or day three of the crisis, you're probably thinking, hmm, maybe I wish I'd put in the solar panels all over the Rajasthan desert. Maybe I wish we'd had a bunch of cheap Chinese EVs and weren't so dependent on Saudi oil and Qatari gas.
So I do think coming out of this, there will be more investment on alternatives for me. That's even for just national strategic reasons. Forget the green. It's just going to be the same story as China. It's just more of everything for national security imperatives. And yes, for the oil services industry, this does sound rather promising. For me, the big question, you mentioned the rebuilding of Iran, which I think will be a a big story. You mentioned the pipelines and the new ports being built in Saudi and in the UAE. One of the big questions, of course, is who's going to do that? Right now, you look at the new pipeline that the UAE is building to Amman. It's being built by the Chinese. Really? It's being built by the Chinese. And so there's a little bit of, if you're Saudi Arabia and you're thinking, I want to build a new pipeline right now. not in two years' time, right now, who has the engineer, who has the steel, who has the tungsten, who has the capacity to come and build this today? That list is pretty short of people who can come in and do it right here, right now. And yeah, so that list is pretty short.
And there's one name on top of it. So that brings me actually to a question that, look, we've got a few questions. You and I are talking about pretty big capital spending booms everywhere, potentially in Iran, across the Middle East, capital spending boom across our countries as we look to redo our alternatives or to just redo our electricity grids on every front, all of which probably was needed anyway, just because of the AI data demand, the electricity demand, et cetera. But now it's the imperative is just as strong. We've got that. We've got every country announcing more money for defense. The U.S. saying, oh, we're going to move from one trillion to one and a half. Europe saying we're going to move from less than 200 billion to 380 billion. And, you know, Middle East, everybody's saying we're going to spend more on defense. Obviously, we've got all the data center spending needs that are just gargantuan numbers. According to McKinsey, it's $6.7 trillion between now and 2030. So it's demand for capital. Like wherever you care to look, it's like, oh, we need money for this and we need money for this and we need money for this.
I mean, this to me sounds like an inflationary boom with rapidly rising real rates, all this demand for capital. And so we have a bunch of questions as to, you know, which quadrant are we going to be in following this peace deal? Where do we end up? Now, usually wars are inflationary. My starting point is wars are inflationary and peace is deflationary, you know, by and large. And that works well. And that's why, you know, my friend Luke's old saying, Luke Roman's old saying that if truth is the first casualty of war, bonds are a close second. So conceptually, we could say, oh, look, it's peace, peace dividend, peace for our time, deflation, buy bonds, happy days. But nothing I'm seeing makes me want to do that. Like absolutely nothing. And and I think we're yeah, I think we're in a full on inflationary boom. I think the risk of an inflationary bust was always that oil goes above 100 bucks. I think one of the lessons from this crisis is that that's less likely to happen. I'm not saying it's never going to happen. I'm saying it's less likely.
And I struggle to see a bust with the U.S. running budget deficits of seven percent of GDP, potentially going to nine with all this money that's being spent everywhere. So like where do you think we are in these quadrants?
Anatole Kaletsky
Well, no, I well, I agree. And that was the conclusion of the piece I wrote this week was that, you know, we we were clearly in the inflationary boom quadrant until February. then there was an understandable concern that we move into the worst part of the whole map, which is the inflationary bust, the rerun of 1974, which means a bear market in everything. It's a point that you made that in that situation, if there was going to be a bear market in equities, it would be accompanied by bear market and bonds and the dollar and everything else. And the war also seemed to preclude the most benign possibility, which is the what we call the disinflationary boom, where growth, you know, accelerates and continues, you know, at above trend. But you don't get get inflationary pressures. Now, with if I'm right, if we're right, that the war has now ended, I think we can completely forget about the inflationary bust scenario for the reasons you've said.
But the interesting question, and where I agree with you, but the market does not agree with either of us, I believe that it's that where we are today makes the continuation of an inflationary boom, much more likely than the really the kind of ideal Goldilocks scenario of moving down from the inflationary boom into the deflationary boom, which is what the markets, apart from bonds, are now against again price for. And I would say even in bonds, and you know, the reason that you're an answer to the in the first part of our discussion, I said there were three big trends that were running before the war, two of which were reversed by the war, but the third one, which was high yields and higher inflation, of course, was not reversed by the by the war. If anything, it was actually, you know, got a turbo charge as a result of the war. But I think that turbo charge continues because the war is not good. The end of the war is not going to be hugely disinflationary for oil prices are not going to collapse back to thirty dollars or fifty dollars. Sure, they'll stabilize. Sure, for months to months, this will improve things.
But the underlying dynamics of inflation, which were already very strong even before you got this additional oil shock, are still going to be there. And if anything, they're going to be re-strengthened now by even more defense spending, even, you know, reconstruction in the Middle East, even more duplication of supply chains because of, you know, the security implications of what we've seen, even more stock building, like you've said, of not just China building up energy and commodity stocks, but, you know, any other country that has big current account surpluses and can afford to. And this is a great point you've been making in our seminars over the last three months. Well, any country that has lots of lots of surplus money, instead of investing the reserves in US treasuries, they're going to invest that in oil stocks and food stocks and mineral stocks. And what is it, the Chinese have a roast, a frozen pork reserve and everything.
Louis-Vincent Gave
It's called a piggy bank.
Anatole Kaletsky
That all actually adds to the inflationary dynamics rather than eliminating it. And that's, interestingly enough, I think, what the markets are concluding. There, I agree, you know, there hasn't been a big improvement in bond yields in the last few days. The Fed has turned out to be perhaps more hawkish than expected. That's the excuse. But the underlying reality is that the inflationary pressure is there and is going to intensify rather than diminish, I think, over the next year or two.
Louis-Vincent Gave
So that brings me to the, we have a few questions on metals, most specifically on gold, on silver. Usually when you're in the inflationary boom quadrant, metals do well. And for the record, you know, I started the year, we will have to put our best trade for the year. My trade was long copper, short Bitcoin, which I still believe in. I still think of all the metals, copper is perhaps the more attractive, even as it stands at an all time high. But switching to gold and silver, we all turned pretty bearish on gold at the start of this war. We published a number of pieces. And for me, the main reason I turned bearish on gold was the belief that, okay, the one you just highlighted saying, okay, central banks are going to be done buying gold for now. Because essentially, when you buy gold, whether you're India or Indonesia or Saudi Arabia, et cetera, you're still buying it on the premise that you can transform your gold into whatever it is that you might need. And that if you need, if you need to turn your gold into wheat, if you need to turn your gold into rice, you can do so pretty easily.
Obviously, the fact that the US Navy no longer controls the world's sea lanes shifted that. And I therefore thought, okay, central banks are going to be done buying gold and they're going to be, like you mentioned, buying up, building up a frozen pork reserve like China does. Now, I think another reason to perhaps be bearish gold, the one espoused by Will, was the fact that the Fed was going to be more hawkish on inflation with the inflationary shock. And when the Fed is hawkish, you typically don't want to be long gold. And that brings us to the current quandary right now, namely that on the one hand, we do have this inflationary environment. On the other hand, we do have an environment where people are going to be most likely rotating out of the US bringing a weaker US dollar, which should be bullish gold. On the other, we have real rates that are likely going to be going up. And real rates are going to be being pushed higher by all this demand for capital that we've discussed. Absolutely enormous demand for capital. So it's a little bit like, okay, which is going to prevail?
Now, to be very clear, I actually think that gold does go higher here. But I think within the metal space, gold might be less interesting than silver and less interesting than copper. Copper is obviously going to be the biggest beneficiary of this massive capex boom, whether in electricity grids and data data center spend, etc. Copper is going to be the biggest beneficiary of that. I think silver is going to be a pretty decent beneficiary of the capex boom in alternatives and solar and whatever else. And then gold. Gold, I think, will benefit from the rotation of private savings away from US dollar assets and into gold, even if the central banks are probably done for a while. So on the question of gold, I think we can assume that the gold bull market will restart. It just might not be the best metal to own. But in an inflationary boom, which is where we seem to be heading, you definitely do not want to be short gold. That's for sure. So hopefully that answers the questions for gold. We seem to have lost Anatole. Hopefully he joins us back soon. As he does, I'll answer. Oh, you're back.
Anatole Kaletsky

So your local guys in China buying local stocks, they're fine. So the question, I think, isn't so much why is China sucking wind as much as why is Hong Kong sucking wind? Because it's really a Hong Kong problem. And I wrote about this in yesterday's daily for those very interested in the question. And I think it comes down to a few factors. But the number one factor has been the reason China's really, really gone down a lot in the past six weeks or so, sorry, eight shares have gone down a lot in the past six weeks or so, is I think is linked partly to the crackdown by the Chinese authorities on Chinese capital outflows through brokers.
Essentially, you remember that in May, the Chinese government slapped pretty consequential fines on some Hong Kong some local brokers, Futu, Tiger, companies that were doing business in China, getting Chinese savings and helping Chinese savings invest and essentially circumvent capital controls and invest either in Hong Kong or in US equities. And this was a practice that essentially went against the rules, but that the government was tolerating and then decided to no longer tolerate. And I think this unleashed a few fears. The first fear was, oh my God, are they going to, so if they're cracking down on the little guys, are they going to crack down on the AIAs and the Manulives and the Ping An insurance that have been selling massive insurance policies to Chinese people also in sort of helping them get around capital controls? And are they going to go for HSBC for standard chartered? Because if they do, then the selling on the Hong Kong stock market really won't stop. And so I think that unleashed that fear.
It also unleashed, I think if you've been a foreign China investor, given your experience of the past 10 years, you have a very high level of PTSD. You remember the education sector crackdown. You remember the Jack Ma slash Alibaba crackdown. And so when you see the Chinese government come in and crack down again, you're like, oh my God, I've seen this movie before. Get me out quick. And so that's been what's unfolding. Now, I think, and we wrote about this, I think what's happened with the local brokers has been a case of the old Chinese saying of kill the chickens to scare the monkeys. So you make an example out of fairly small and inconsequential. I mean, not to put down these companies, they're not inconsequential companies, but in the whole scheme of things, they're not systemic risk. It's not like going after HSBC or Standard Chartered or AIA. You go for small players to essentially put AIA and Standard Chartered and HSBC on notice to make them stop doing this. So the end result now, why would China do that? And I think the end result is the reason China's doing this is pretty simple. They actually want the A-shares to go up.
I think that much was very visible when they intervened in 24, when they intervened again in 25 at the time of the Liberation Day tariffs. The Chinese government wants local shares to go up and they want local shares to go up because that's one of the ways you can repair consumer confidence, which is today the biggest problem China has. If you look on paper, China today has the lowest cost of capital in the world. It has the lowest cost of labor by far, and it has the lowest cost of electricity. So on paper, the place should be going absolutely gangbusters. When you've got the lowest cost of electricity, lowest cost of labor, lowest cost of capital, there's really no constraint to your growth. You should be growing enormously, but you're not because business and consumer confidence have been crushed. And they remain crushed. So the question is, how do you fix this? And one of the ways perhaps you fix this is by allowing local stock markets to go up. And so I think what's being directed is very much, okay, we don't have our savings to go abroad. We want them to keep staying here and going higher here.
Now, the flip side of all of this, one that few people mentioned, is that this focus on getting domestic asset prices higher on keeping savings at home also means that the renminbi continues to go up. And for me, if I look back at the big lessons of this crisis, one of the lessons was how China keeps the oil price into a band. The other is that in the past, each time there was uncertainty in the world, each time the local authorities in China froze the exchange rate. They would just, you see it literally every time, the renminbi just goes flat as a pancake against the US dollar. Not this time. Here, the renminbi coming into this crisis was going up 40 to 50 basis points a month. And it's still going up 40 to 50 basis points a month. It kept going up 40, 50 basis points a month during this crisis, which meant that China was the only bond market that actually delivered positive returns through this only major bond market that delivered positive returns through through this through this Iran war. Once again, Chinese government bonds were the diversification for portfolios that US treasuries, German boons, JGB, etc. were not.
And this time around, it wasn't so much because of collapsing yields as much as a very strongly rising currency. So so I think that's been part of the explanation for Hong Kong. I think the other explanation is that Hong Kong, in a sense, is suffering some of the same woos as the US market, where if you look at the US market, you've had this huge dichotomy where, as you point out, everything linked to semiconductors, everything linked to tech hardware is ripped higher. And that's been funded a lot by people selling software stocks and other tech stocks. And, you know, you pointed out that the Mac seven have gone nowhere. It lasted basically exactly 40 years till 22. And now we've been four years into an uptrend, but maybe we're that suggests maybe we're only a 10th of the way, you know, so we can't come to any confident conclusions on the basis of any of the experience, literally that we've had even people as old as me over 70 have had in our working lifetimes. So having said that, you know, what can you look at, you can't really look at history. So you can just look at sort of common sense and economic theory. And that suggests that if we're having an investment boom, which we are, especially in the US, real interest rates are going to have to go up because there's going there's got to be crowding out.
I mean, all this enormous investment that's happening in technology infrastructure and hyper scaling either will crash very, very soon, and it probably won't. But the classic thing that we're also crowd out would be government borrowing, you know, that you'd have higher growth, higher incomes, generating smaller deficits. But that's not happening. And our view has been consistently that it's better to take credit risk than duration risk. And I think that view is now renewed, actually, after the war. Now, you know, if the war had continued, or if I'm wrong, and if it comes back, and the world goes into a slump in 2027, then obviously, credit spreads will widen, there'll be lots of defaults and so on. Well, you know, I think that Japan is economically, leaving aside the markets, is, I think, unquestionably in a better place now than it's been at any time since the late 1980s. So for Japan, the regime change, which, you know, is happening, if you, that's our kind of big theme, the once in a lifetime regime change that's happening now in a negative direction in the US, in Europe, in much of the world in terms of higher inflation, higher interest rates, so for Japan, it's the other way around. They're actually emerging from 30 years of deflation, stagnation, and so on. They've still got, you know, demographics that are contracting and so on. But Japan's in a good place. The fiscal policy is now much more sensible in that it recognizes a long-term, but crucially, long-term need for fiscal stability, if not fiscal consolidation, but it also recognizes that fiscal policy has a big role to play in demand management.
So that's what, and they're running a fiscally fairly loose policy, which I think is right, and is good, and it's actually not bad for their bond market, because it will stabilize the economy, but also, they're clearly going to have a defense spending boom, because, like everybody else, they're realizing you can't rely on the American security umbrella. And actually, Japan, I think, objectively, is in a much more dangerous part of the world than, for instance, the Europeans are. There's no way that Russia is going to attack Western Europe, whereas you could imagine a situation where China would attack at least part of Japan. There's got to be a lot more restructuring of the infrastructure, electrification, and all that kind of thing. So I think Japan, and meanwhile, you know, deflation is over. Okay, so it's massively undervalued, and monetary and fiscal policy are now moving in the direction that normally strengthens currencies.
You're moving to a looser fiscal, tighter monetary position. Now, people say, well, they're not moving fast enough. Well, but they are clearly moving in the right direction, and relative to the last 30 years, they're moving very, very fast. So I think it basically comes down to trends. You know, markets are trend-following most of the time. The currency markets in the short term are the most trend-following, momentum-driven markets of all.
